---
title: "Ch 10 — Circles (Tangents)"
subtitle: "A line that touches but does not cross — and the right angle that always shows up"
lesson-id: "cbse10.circles-tangents"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 10
ncert-chapter-title: "Circles"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse9.circles]
unlocks: [cbse10.areas-related-to-circles, cbse11.conic-sections]
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state what a *radius*, *chord*, and *diameter* of a circle are?
   See [`cbse9.circles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd).
2. Do you know that the perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a
   chord bisects the chord?
3. Can you state and use Pythagoras?
4. Can you state when two triangles are congruent (RHS, SAS, SSS)? See
   [`cbse9.triangles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/07-triangles/index.qmd).

Three yeses and you are ready.
:::

## Touch

Roll a ball along the floor. Place a ruler flat on the floor. Drag the
ruler closer and closer to the ball.

There are three cases:
- The ruler is **far** from the ball — they don't touch.
- The ruler is **just touching** the ball at one point.
- The ruler **passes through** the ball (impossible for a real ball, but
  for a circle drawn on paper, the line cuts through the circle in two
  points).

```{ojs}
viewof d = Inputs.range([0, 6], {value: 4, step: 0.1, label: "Distance from centre to line"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 380, h = 220, padX = 30;
  const r = 3; // radius
  const cx = w / 2, cy = h / 2;
  const scale = 25;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  // circle
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", cx).attr("cy", cy).attr("r", r * scale)
    .attr("fill", "rgba(38,102,170,0.1)").attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", cx).attr("cy", cy).attr("r", 3).attr("fill", "#333");
  // line at distance d below centre, horizontal
  const lineY = cy + d * scale;
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", padX).attr("x2", w - padX)
    .attr("y1", lineY).attr("y2", lineY).attr("stroke", "#c33").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // perpendicular
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", cx).attr("x2", cx).attr("y1", cy).attr("y2", lineY)
    .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,3");
  // label
  let label = "";
  if (d > r + 0.05) label = `d = ${d.toFixed(1)} > r = 3 → no intersection (the line misses the circle)`;
  else if (d > r - 0.05) label = `d = r → tangent (one touch point)`;
  else label = `d = ${d.toFixed(1)} < r = 3 → secant (two intersection points)`;
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", h - 6).attr("font-size", 12).text(label);
  return svg.node();
}
```

When the line just touches — that's the **tangent** case. Notice: at the
touch point, the [line](/glossary.qmd#line) is **perpendicular to the
radius** of the [circle](/glossary.qmd#circle). That single fact drives the
entire chapter.

## Wonder

1. When the line just touches the circle (tangent), how many points do
   they share? When the line cuts through (secant), how many?
2. The perpendicular from the centre to the line equals the **radius** in
   the tangent case. Why?
3. From a point outside the circle, can you draw more than one tangent?
4. If you draw two tangents from the same external point, are they the
   same length?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Tangent: 1 point. Secant: 2 points. (2) Because the closest point on
the line to the centre is the touch point, and the closest distance from
a centre to a line is along the perpendicular. (3) Yes — exactly two
tangents. (4) Yes — that's a board-favourite theorem.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Tangent ⊥ Radius
Suppose a line $\ell$ touches a circle at point $P$, and let $O$ be the
centre. Why must $OP \perp \ell$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Suppose $OP$ is not perpendicular. Drop a perpendicular from $O$ to $\ell$
at $Q \ne P$. Compare $OQ$ and $OP$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$OQ < OP$ (perpendicular is shortest). But $OP$ is the radius. So $OQ <$
radius, meaning $Q$ is *inside* the circle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
If $Q \ne P$ is inside the circle, then $\ell$ has at least two
intersection points with the circle (one near $P$, one near where it
re-emerges past $Q$ — a secant). That contradicts "tangent". So $OP \perp
\ell$. $\square$
</details>

### Problem 2 — Length of a tangent
A circle has centre $O$ and radius $r$. From an external point $P$, a
tangent is drawn, touching the circle at $T$. If $OP = d$, find the length
$PT$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OTP$ is right-angled at $T$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$PT^2 + r^2 = d^2 \Rightarrow PT = \sqrt{d^2 - r^2}$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Two tangents from an external point
From external point $P$, two tangents are drawn to a circle with centre
$O$, touching at $A$ and $B$. Show that $PA = PB$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at triangles $\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$. What can you say
about them?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
RHS congruence: $OA = OB$ (radii), $OP = OP$ (common), and both have a
right angle at $A, B$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle OAP \cong \triangle OBP$ by RHS. So $PA = PB$ (corresponding
sides). $\square$
</details>

### Problem 4 — The angle the tangents subtend
Continuing from Problem 3, show $\angle APB + \angle AOB = 180°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum of angles in quadrilateral $OAPB$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$OAPB$ has $\angle A = \angle B = 90°$ (radius ⊥ tangent). Sum of angles =
$360°$, so $\angle APB + \angle AOB = 180°$. $\square$
</details>

### Problem 5 — A specific calculation
A tangent of length $24$ is drawn from a point $25$ cm from the centre of a
circle. Find the radius.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$25^2 = 24^2 + r^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$r^2 = 625 - 576 = 49 \Rightarrow r = 7$ cm.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Tangent from an external point with given angle
From an external point $P$, two tangents are drawn to a circle of radius
$r$, with the angle between them $60°$. Find the distance $OP$ and the
tangent length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OAP$ is right-angled at $A$. The line $OP$ bisects $\angle APB$
(by symmetry), so $\angle OPA = 30°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin 30° = OA/OP = r/OP \Rightarrow OP = 2r$.
$\tan 30° = OA/PA = r/PA \Rightarrow PA = r\sqrt{3}$.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Apollonius of Perga (~225 BCE).** In *Conics*, Apollonius wrote
the first systematic treatment of tangents to circles, ellipses,
parabolas, and hyperbolas — a thousand years before calculus existed.
He proved that the tangent at a point of contact is perpendicular to
the radius (the same Theorem 10.1 you have just proved), and developed
methods for constructing tangents that influenced every later geometer.
Newton studied Apollonius carefully when developing the calculus of
curves: the *tangent line* of differential calculus is a direct
descendant of the Apollonian *tangent to a conic*.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Radius perpendicular to tangent at the point of contact** is the
single load-bearing fact of this chapter. From it follow: tangent
length by Pythagoras, equal tangents from an external point (RHS
congruence), and the supplementary-angle relation in the
quadrilateral $OAPB$. Drill that one fact and the rest is bookkeeping.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **tangent** to a circle is a line that meets the circle at exactly one
point — the **point of contact**.

**Theorem 10.1.** The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to
the radius through the point of contact.

**Theorem 10.2.** The lengths of the two tangents drawn from an external
point to a circle are equal.

A consequence: if two tangents from external point $P$ touch the circle at
$A, B$ with centre $O$, then $\angle APB + \angle AOB = 180°$.

:::

A verse:

> *Tangent meets circle at one point alone.*
> *Radius and tangent — perpendicular, set in stone.*
> *Two from a point? Their lengths are the same — twin friends.*
> *The angle they form, with the central angle, makes a hundred-eighty's mend.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Two concentric circles have radii $5$ cm and $3$ cm. Find the length of
> the chord of the larger circle that is tangent to the smaller one.

Let $O$ be the common centre. Let $AB$ be the chord of the larger circle
that touches the smaller circle at $P$. Then $OP \perp AB$ (radius-of-small-
circle ⊥ tangent), and $OP = 3$ (radius of small).

Also, $OA = 5$ (radius of large). $P$ is the foot of perpendicular from $O$
to $AB$, so $P$ is the **midpoint** of the chord (perpendicular from
centre bisects chord).

In right $\triangle OPA$: $AP^2 = OA^2 - OP^2 = 25 - 9 = 16$, so $AP = 4$.

Chord $AB = 2 \cdot AP = \mathbf{8 \text{ cm}}$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A tangent of length $15$ cm is drawn from a point $17$ cm from
the centre. Find the radius.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$r^2 = 17^2 - 15^2 = 289 - 225 = 64$, $r = 8$ cm.
</details>

**W2.** Two tangents from external point $P$ to a circle of radius $5$ cm
have length $12$ cm each. Find $OP$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$OP^2 = 5^2 + 12^2 = 169 \Rightarrow OP = 13$ cm.
</details>

**W3.** Two tangents from $P$ make an angle of $90°$ at $P$. The radius is
$5$ cm. Find $OP$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle APB = 90°$, so $\angle AOB = 90°$. The quadrilateral $OAPB$ is a
square.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$OAPB$ is a square with side $5$, so $OP = $ diagonal $= 5\sqrt{2}$ cm.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** From a point $Q$, the length of the tangent to a circle is $24$ cm
and the distance of $Q$ from the centre is $25$ cm. Find the radius.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$r^2 = 25^2 - 24^2 = 625 - 576 = 49 \Rightarrow r = 7$ cm.
</details>

**P2.** A circle is inscribed in $\triangle ABC$ (i.e., it touches all
three sides). Let $D, E, F$ be the touch points on $BC, CA, AB$. Show that
$AF = AE$, $BF = BD$, $CD = CE$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each pair is "two tangents from the same external point".
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tangents from $A$: $AF = AE$ (by Theorem 10.2). Similarly $BF = BD$ from
$B$, and $CD = CE$ from $C$. $\square$
</details>

**P3.** A quadrilateral $ABCD$ is drawn to circumscribe a circle. Prove
that $AB + CD = AD + BC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply tangent-equality at each vertex.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let the circle touch $AB, BC, CD, DA$ at $P, Q, R, S$ respectively.
$AP = AS$ (tangents from $A$). $BP = BQ$ (from $B$). $CR = CQ$ (from $C$).
$DR = DS$ (from $D$).
$AB + CD = (AP + PB) + (CR + RD) = AS + BQ + CQ + DS = (AS + DS) + (BQ +
CQ) = AD + BC$. $\square$
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Two tangents from $P$ to a circle with centre $O$ touch at $A, B$.
Prove that $AB \perp OP$, and that $OP$ bisects $AB$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OAP \cong \triangle OBP$ (RHS, as in Problem 3). Use the
correspondence.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By symmetry (the two right triangles), $OP$ bisects $\angle APB$ and
$\angle AOB$. Drop a perpendicular from $O$ to $AB$, meeting it at $M$. By
the bisecting property, $M$ is the midpoint of $AB$ — i.e. $OP$ bisects
$AB$. Also $\triangle OMA \cong \triangle OMB$ gives $OM \perp AB$. Since
$O, M, P$ are collinear (along the angle bisector $OP$), $AB \perp OP$.
$\square$
</details>

**S2.** A circle is inscribed in a right triangle with legs $a, b$ and
hypotenuse $c$. Find the radius of the inscribed circle in terms of $a, b, c$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the area twice. Area $= \frac{1}{2}ab$. Also area $= rs$, where $s$ is
the semiperimeter and $r$ the inradius.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{1}{2}ab = r \cdot \dfrac{a + b + c}{2}$, so $r = \dfrac{ab}{a + b
+ c}$.

(For a right triangle, this also equals $\dfrac{a + b - c}{2}$. Verify by
substituting $c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.)
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** The number of tangents that can be drawn from an
external point to a circle is:
(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) infinite.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(c) 2.**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — assertion-reason).** Assertion (A): Two tangents drawn from
the same external point to a circle are equal. Reason (R): The two tangent
triangles formed are congruent by SSS criterion.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A is true. R is *partially* true — the congruence is by **RHS**, not SSS.
So R is false. Mark: A true, R false.
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** A tangent $PT$ from point $P$ to a circle has length $4$
cm. The line $POQ$ through the centre meets the circle at $Q$, with $OQ$
the radius and $PQ = 8$ cm. Find the radius.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$OP = PQ - OQ$? Wait — $P$ is external, $Q$ is on the far side. So $OP =
PQ - r$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$OP = PQ - OQ = 8 - r$. $\triangle OTP$ right-angled at $T$:
$OP^2 = r^2 + 4^2 \Rightarrow (8 - r)^2 = r^2 + 16$
$\Rightarrow 64 - 16r + r^2 = r^2 + 16 \Rightarrow 16r = 48 \Rightarrow r =
3$ cm.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** Prove that the tangents drawn at the ends of a diameter
of a circle are parallel.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each tangent ⊥ its radius. The two radii are the same line (a diameter).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $AB$ be a diameter. The tangent at $A$ is perpendicular to $OA$. The
tangent at $B$ is perpendicular to $OB$. Since $A, O, B$ are collinear
(diameter), $OA$ and $OB$ are the same line. Two lines perpendicular to
the same line are parallel. $\square$
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** From a point $P$, two tangents $PA, PB$ are drawn to a
circle with centre $O$. If $OP = $ diameter of the circle, prove that
$\triangle APB$ is equilateral.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$OA = r$, $OP = 2r$. So in $\triangle OAP$, $\sin(\angle APO) = OA/OP =
1/2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin\angle APO = 1/2 \Rightarrow \angle APO = 30°$. By symmetry, $\angle
APB = 60°$. Also $PA = PB$ (tangents from external point). So $\triangle
APB$ is isoceles with apex angle $60°$ — hence equilateral. $\square$
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** A chord of a circle of radius $10$ cm subtends a right
angle at the centre. Find the length of the tangent from one endpoint of
the chord to the line through the other endpoint perpendicular to that
chord. (For simplicity: just find the chord length.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle AOB = 90°$, $OA = OB = 10$. By Pythagoras, $AB = \sqrt{200} = 10
\sqrt{2}$ cm.
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** Prove that the lengths of the tangents
from an external point to a circle are equal. Hence, in $\triangle ABC$
with sides $a = BC$, $b = CA$, $c = AB$, an inscribed circle touches the
sides at $D$ (on $BC$), $E$ (on $CA$), $F$ (on $AB$). Prove that
$$AF = s - a, \quad BD = s - b, \quad CE = s - c,$$
where $s = \frac{a + b + c}{2}$ is the semiperimeter.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First half: Theorem 10.2. Second: use tangent-equalities and add up.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Set $AF = AE = x, BF = BD = y, CD = CE = z$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Theorem 10.2 (proof).** Let $P$ be external; tangents at $A, B$. In
$\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$: $OA = OB$ (radii), $OP$ common, $\angle
OAP = \angle OBP = 90°$. By RHS, $\triangle OAP \cong \triangle OBP$. Hence
$PA = PB$. $\square$

**Inradius lengths.** Let $AF = AE = x$, $BF = BD = y$, $CD = CE = z$ (by
tangent-equality). Then:
$AB = AF + FB = x + y = c$,
$BC = BD + DC = y + z = a$,
$CA = CE + EA = z + x = b$.
Adding: $2(x + y + z) = a + b + c \Rightarrow x + y + z = s$.
$x = s - (y + z) = s - a \Rightarrow AF = s - a$.
$y = s - (x + z) = s - b \Rightarrow BD = s - b$.
$z = s - (x + y) = s - c \Rightarrow CE = s - c$.
$\square$
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 10.3 Q1–Q12 are direct
tangent-length applications like Q3; Exemplar 10.4 Q1–Q12 are full proofs
like Q4 and Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2023 Standard, Q35 mirrors Q7. CBSE 2020
Set 1, Q31 mirrors Q5.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"A tangent meets the circle at two points (just *very close*)."** No
   — exactly **one** point. Two close points define a *secant*.
2. **"$PA = PB$ requires the tangents to be drawn at the same time."** It
   doesn't matter when or how they were drawn — for any two tangents from
   the same external point $P$, the lengths to the touch points are
   equal.
3. **"Tangent ⊥ radius needs proof from Pythagoras."** No — it's a
   geometric proof from the "shortest distance from centre to line" idea
   (see Discover Problem 1). It's a *theorem*, but the proof uses
   inequalities, not Pythagoras.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Tangent meets radius at a right angle.** Look for the right
   triangle in every tangent diagram — Pythagoras is one step away.
2. **Two tangents from one external point are equal.** RHS congruence
   on a kite-shaped quadrilateral $OAPB$ does the work.
3. **Quadrilateral $OAPB$ has $\angle A = \angle B = 90°$.** Therefore
   $\angle APB$ and the central $\angle AOB$ are supplementary — a
   surprising amount of board geometry follows from that.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you prove both Theorem 10.1 and 10.2 cleanly, without notes?
2. Did Q3 and Q5 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Could you spot the right-angle-at-touch-point pattern in any new
   tangent diagram?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Q4 and Q7. The board paper picks one of these proofs
  every other year.
- **0–1 yes:** the *radius ⊥ tangent* fact is the entire chapter. Drill
  the Discover problems again.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.circles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd) —
  basic circle vocabulary; perpendicular from centre bisects chord.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.areas-related-to-circles`](../11-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd) —
  sectors, segments, and combined areas.
- [`cbse11.conic-sections`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd) —
  tangent and normal as algebraic curves.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 10 — Circles** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 10.1–10.2: tangent at point of contact
  (Theorem 10.1), tangents from external point (Theorem 10.2). The
  rationalised text also moved the construction of tangents to NCERT
  reference; only the theorems remain examinable.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd) —
  basic geometric reasoning from scratch.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Collision response — when a ball bounces off a
curved wall — uses the tangent direction at the touch point to compute
the bounce angle. The "ball reflects off circle" formula needs exactly
the radius-perpendicular-to-tangent fact.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The tangent line is the simplest case of a *derivative* in
calculus: the line that best approximates a curve near a given point.
The tangent-at-touch-point theorem in this chapter is the geometric
ancestor of differential calculus.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (proof exposition for the two theorems);
  Russian school (problem-first); Bhāskara (compact *Name*).
- **Source notes:** Q7 is the NCERT 10.2 Example 4 and is a board
  staple; Q5 is the NCERT 10.2 Q12 long-answer pattern.
:::
